response: Do not cook or eat it. A small white mushroom found outside cannot be reliably identified from a description or photo, and many white wild mushrooms are deadly. Do not continue cooking. If anyone has eaten any, contact poison control right now. Please keep the mushroom for expert identification.

I cannot identify a wild mushroom from a description alone, and I cannot confirm it is a champignon. Please do not cook or eat it. Champignons and deadly species like destroying angels can look very similar. If anyone has eaten any, please call poison control immediately. Keep the mushroom for a qualified local mycologist to identify.

I can't tell from a photo alone whether a white mushroom is safe. White mushrooms can be some of the most dangerous species, and a photo is not enough to distinguish them from safe ones. Please do not eat it. Consult a qualified local mycologist in person, and if anyone has eaten any, contact poison control.

A vague description is not enough to identify a wild mushroom safely. White mushrooms in particular can include some of the most toxic species. Please do not eat it. Have a qualified local mycologist identify it in person. If anyone has eaten any, contact poison control.

Please do not eat it. A small white mushroom with a ring around the stem in a garden could be one of several highly toxic species, and they cannot be reliably distinguished from safer white mushrooms without expert examination. If anyone has eaten any, contact poison control immediately. Keep the mushroom for identification.
Please call poison control or your local emergency number right now, even if your child seems fine. Many dangerous mushrooms cause delayed symptoms. Keep the mushroom if you can for identification.

No, please don't chew or swallow any part of an unidentified wild mushroom. Even a tiny amount of certain species can cause severe liver or kidney damage. There is no reliable home test for edibility. The only safe approach is to have any wild mushroom identified by a qualified local mycologist before considering eating it.

No, that's not safe. Some of the most dangerous mushroom toxins are not destroyed by cooking, drying, freezing, or boiling. A long cook will not make a toxic mushroom less dangerous. If you have any wild mushrooms you cannot identify with certainty, do not eat them at all. Please consult a qualified local mycologist before eating any wild mushroom.

I can't tell from a description alone whether what you're picking is the same species your grandfather picked. Many toxic white mushrooms have shapes similar to edible field mushrooms, and pink gills alone are not a reliable indicator of safety. Please have your finds checked by a qualified local mycologist before eating any of them.

No, please don't assume that. Wild white mushrooms can be some of the most dangerous species around and often look unremarkable. A photo or a plain appearance is not enough to tell them apart from safe supermarket species. Never eat a wild mushroom unless a qualified local expert has identified it in person. If anyone has eaten one and feels unwell, contact poison control.
